title: Tang Yuan Glutinous Rice Balls
description: Tang Yuan, these delicate glutinous rice balls, are a quintessential Chinese dessert, cherished for their smooth, chewy texture and rich, sweet fillings. Often enjoyed during festive occasions like the Lantern Festival and Winter Solstice, they symbolize reunion and completeness. This recipe guides you to create perfectly soft, tender spheres bursting with a luscious black sesame paste, served in a comforting ginger-infused syrup.

ingredients.notes: melted, or unsalted butter/coconut oil, for filling
approx. 180ml, adjust as needed, for dough
or granulated sugar, to taste, for syrup

instructions.title: Prepare the Black Sesame Filling
instructions.text: If not pre-roasted, toast black sesame seeds in a dry pan over medium-low heat until fragrant, about 3-5 minutes. Let cool completely. Grind the roasted sesame seeds with granulated sugar in a food processor or spice grinder until a fine powder forms. Add the melted lard (or butter/coconut oil) and process until it forms a thick, cohesive paste. Transfer to a bowl, cover, and chill in the refrigerator for at least 30 minutes to firm up. Once firm, roll the paste into small balls, about 1/2-inch (1.25 cm) in diameter. You should yield about 25-30 balls.

instructions.title: Make the Glutinous Rice Dough
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, gradually add warm water to the glutinous rice flour, mixing with chopsticks or your hands. Continue adding water a little at a time until a soft, pliable, and non-sticky dough forms. The dough should be smooth and easy to handle. If it's too dry, add a tiny bit more water; if too wet, add a little more flour. Knead for 2-3 minutes until uniform.

instructions.title: Assemble the Tang Yuan
instructions.text: Divide the dough into small, equal portions, about 1-inch (2.5 cm) in diameter. Flatten each portion into a round disc, creating a slightly thicker edge and a thinner center. Place one chilled black sesame filling ball in the center of the dough disc. Gently gather the edges of the dough around the filling and pinch them together to seal completely. Roll the filled dough between your palms to form a smooth, round ball. Place the finished Tang Yuan on a lightly floured plate to prevent sticking. Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.

instructions.title: Prepare the Ginger Syrup
instructions.text: In a medium saucepan, combine 4 cups of water, smashed ginger slices, and rock sugar. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, then reduce heat to low and simmer for 10-15 minutes, allowing the ginger flavor to infuse and the sugar to dissolve completely. Taste and adjust sweetness if desired. Keep warm.

instructions.title: Cook the Tang Yuan
instructions.text: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Carefully drop the Tang Yuan into the boiling water, one by one, ensuring not to overcrowd the pot. Gently stir occasionally to prevent them from sticking to the bottom. Once the Tang Yuan float to the surface, continue to cook for another 1-2 minutes until they appear plump and translucent.

instructions.title: Serve Immediately
instructions.text: Using a slotted spoon, carefully transfer the cooked Tang Yuan directly into individual serving bowls. Ladle the warm ginger syrup over the Tang Yuan, ensuring each bowl gets a few slices of ginger. Serve immediately while warm to enjoy their optimal soft and chewy texture.

tips: For perfectly smooth Tang Yuan, ensure your dough is well-kneaded and pliable. If it cracks during shaping, it's too dry; add a few drops of water.
Chilling the black sesame filling is crucial. It makes the paste firm enough to roll into balls, preventing it from oozing out during shaping.
When cooking, do not overcrowd the pot. Cook in batches if necessary to maintain water temperature and prevent sticking, ensuring even cooking.
article:
## History of Tang Yuan Glutinous Rice Balls

Tang Yuan (湯圓) literally means "soup balls" and is a traditional Chinese dessert made from glutinous rice flour. Its round shape symbolizes completeness and reunion (團圓, tuányuán), making it a significant dish during family gatherings, especially the Lantern Festival (元宵節, Yuánxiāojié) and Winter Solstice (冬至, Dōngzhì). While often associated with sweet fillings like black sesame, red bean, or peanut, savory versions also exist in some regions. The practice of eating Tang Yuan dates back to the Song Dynasty, evolving into a beloved custom celebrating togetherness and good fortune.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe masterfully balances texture and flavor. The use of warm water for the glutinous rice flour creates a dough that is incredibly soft, pliable, and yields a delightfully chewy Tang Yuan when cooked, without being overly sticky. The black sesame filling, enriched with a touch of lard (or butter), offers a deeply aromatic, nutty, and subtly sweet contrast to the mild rice dough. The simple yet fragrant ginger syrup cuts through the richness, adding a refreshing warmth that elevates the entire dessert, ensuring each bite is a harmonious blend of chewiness, sweetness, and warmth.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

Achieving perfect Tang Yuan requires attention to detail. For the filling, ensure black sesame seeds are freshly roasted and finely ground for maximum aroma. Chilling the filling thoroughly before rolling into balls is essential for easy handling and preventing leakage during shaping. When making the dough, add water gradually; the ideal consistency is soft and pliable, not sticky. If the dough cracks, it's too dry; if it's too soft to hold its shape, add a little more flour. When sealing, pinch firmly to avoid the filling escaping during cooking. Lastly, serve immediately after cooking to enjoy their optimal soft and chewy texture.

## Serving Suggestions

Tang Yuan is traditionally served warm in the ginger-infused syrup. For an added layer of flavor and texture, you can garnish with a sprinkle of toasted shredded coconut, a few goji berries, or even a touch of osmanthus flowers. While perfect on its own, a small bowl of Tang Yuan makes an excellent conclusion to a hearty Chinese meal, especially during colder months or festive celebrations. It can also be served alongside a light tea for a comforting afternoon treat.

jsonLd.description: Tang Yuan, these delicate glutinous rice balls, are a quintessential Chinese dessert, cherished for their smooth, chewy texture and rich, sweet fillings. Often enjoyed during festive occasions like the Lantern Festival and Winter Solstice, they symbolize reunion and completeness. This recipe guides you to create perfectly soft, tender spheres bursting with a luscious black sesame paste, served in a comforting ginger-infused syrup.
jsonLd.keywords: Chinese, Chinese, authentic, traditional, desserts, Chinese, dessert, tang yuan, glutinous rice balls, black sesame, traditional, sweet, reunion
recipeIngredient: 1/2 cup Black sesame seeds (roasted, for filling)
2 tbsp Lard (melted, or unsalted butter/coconut oil, for filling)
2 cups Glutinous rice flour (approx. 250g, for dough)
3/4 cup Warm water (approx. 180ml, adjust as needed, for dough)
1/2 cup Rock sugar (or granulated sugar, to taste, for syrup)

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare the Black Sesame Filling
recipeInstructions.text: If not pre-roasted, toast black sesame seeds in a dry pan over medium-low heat until fragrant, about 3-5 minutes. Let cool completely. Add the melted lard (or butter/coconut oil) and process until it forms a thick, cohesive paste. Transfer to a bowl, cover, and chill in the refrigerator for at least 30 minutes to firm up. Once firm, roll the paste into small balls, about 1/2-inch (1.25 cm) in diameter. You should yield about 25-30 balls.

recipeInstructions.name: Make the Glutinous Rice Dough
recipeInstructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, gradually add warm water to the glutinous rice flour, mixing with chopsticks or your hands. Continue adding water a little at a time until a soft, pliable, and non-sticky dough forms. The dough should be smooth and easy to handle. If it's too dry, add a tiny bit more water; if too wet, add a little more flour. Knead for 2-3 minutes until uniform.

recipeInstructions.name: Assemble the Tang Yuan
recipeInstructions.text: Divide the dough into small, equal portions, about 1-inch (2.5 cm) in diameter. Flatten each portion into a round disc, creating a slightly thicker edge and a thinner center. Place one chilled black sesame filling ball in the center of the dough disc. Gently gather the edges of the dough around the filling and pinch them together to seal completely. Roll the filled dough between your palms to form a smooth, round ball. Place the finished Tang Yuan on a lightly floured plate to prevent sticking. Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare the Ginger Syrup
recipeInstructions.text: In a medium saucepan, combine 4 cups of water, smashed ginger slices, and rock sugar. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, then reduce heat to low and simmer for 10-15 minutes, allowing the ginger flavor to infuse and the sugar to dissolve completely. Taste and adjust sweetness if desired. Keep warm.

recipeInstructions.name: Cook the Tang Yuan
recipeInstructions.text: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Carefully drop the Tang Yuan into the boiling water, one by one, ensuring not to overcrowd the pot. Gently stir occasionally to prevent them from sticking to the bottom. Once the Tang Yuan float to the surface, continue to cook for another 1-2 minutes until they appear plump and translucent.

recipeInstructions.name: Serve Immediately
recipeInstructions.text: Using a slotted spoon, carefully transfer the cooked Tang Yuan directly into individual serving bowls. Ladle the warm ginger syrup over the Tang Yuan, ensuring each bowl gets a few slices of ginger. Serve immediately while warm to enjoy their optimal soft and chewy texture.
